f-strings-and-formatting.label: F-Strings and Formatting
f-strings-and-formatting.summary: Create readable output with f-strings and formatted values.

quiz2.title: Which of the following are valid ways to format strings in Python?
quiz2.hint: Consider the different string formatting methods available.

help.concept: Python supports multiple string formatting techniques.
help.hint_1: F-strings are one method, but there are others.
help.hint_2: Think about the `format()` method and `%` formatting.

policy_single_choice_1.title: Identify the result of running code
policy_single_choice_1.prompt: In "F-Strings and Formatting", what should you check after running a Python program?
policy_single_choice_1.hint: Running code lets you inspect what the program displays.

help.concept: When you run Python code, the most important feedback is usually the output or error message.
help.hint_1: Look at the console or output area.
help.hint_2: Printed values appear after the program runs.

options.a: The output shown by the program

policy_multi_choice_1.title: Choose good run-and-check habits
policy_multi_choice_1.prompt: Which habits help when practicing "F-Strings and Formatting"?
policy_multi_choice_1.hint: Good practice means running code and checking what happened.

help.concept: Running small pieces of code and reading the output helps you understand Python behavior.
help.hint_1: Output and errors both give useful feedback.
help.hint_2: Testing small changes is easier than debugging many changes at once.

options.a: Run the code after writing it
options.d: Change many lines at once without testing

sketch1.title: Introduction to F-Strings
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
F-strings are a way to embed expressions inside string literals, using curly braces `{}`. For example, if you have a variable `name = 'Alice'`, you can create a greeting with f-strings like this:

```python
name = 'Alice'
print(f'Hello, {name}!')
```
This will output `Hello, Alice!`. Try it yourself by changing the name!

Line by line: read each statement from top to bottom. Notice which names are created, which values are passed into functions, and what output the code is meant to show.

sketch2.title: Using Format Method
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
Another way to format strings is by using the `format()` method. For example:

```python
age = 30
print('I am {} years old.'.format(age))
```
This will output `I am 30 years old.`. Experiment with different values for `age`!

Line by line: read each statement from top to bottom. Notice which names are created, which values are passed into functions, and what output the code is meant to show.
